services.name: USPTO Trademark Registration
services.shortDesc: Protect your brand with USPTO trademark registration. Required for Amazon Brand Registry. Stop copycats and counterfeiters.
services.metaTitle: US Trademark Registration — USPTO Filing | LLCPad
services.metaDescription: Register your trademark with the USPTO. Protect your brand name & logo in the US. Full filing service for international business owners. Start today.
services.description:
US trademark registration with the USPTO (United States Patent and Trademark Office) gives you exclusive legal rights to your brand name, logo, or slogan across all 50 states. For international entrepreneurs selling in the US market, a registered trademark prevents competitors from using your brand and is essential for platforms like Amazon Brand Registry.

Why You Need a Trademark

  • Nationwide Brand Protection: A federal trademark gives you exclusive rights to your brand name in your registered class of goods or services across the entire United States.
  • Amazon Brand Registry: You must have a registered (or pending) US trademark to enroll in Amazon Brand Registry, which unlocks A+ Content, brand analytics, and counterfeit protection.
  • Legal Enforcement: With a registered trademark, you can file cease-and-desist letters, DMCA takedowns, and federal lawsuits against infringers — and recover statutory damages.
  • Asset Value: A registered trademark is an intangible asset that increases your business valuation and can be licensed or sold independently of the business.
Feature RecommendedTrademark Copyright Patent
What It Protects Brand names, logos, slogans Creative works (books, art, code) Inventions & processes
Duration Indefinite (renew every 10 yrs) Life + 70 years 20 years
Filing Cost (USPTO) $250–$350 per class $45–$65 $1,600+
Filing Authority USPTO US Copyright Office USPTO
Processing Time 8–12 months 1–6 months 1–3 years

How It Works

  1. Trademark Search: We conduct a comprehensive search of the USPTO database and common law sources to check if your desired brand name or logo is available for registration.
  2. File Your Application: Our team prepares and files your trademark application with the USPTO, selecting the correct international class(es) for your goods or services.
  3. Monitor & Respond: We track your application through the USPTO examination process, respond to any Office Actions from the examining attorney, and guide you through publication and registration.

Important: Non-US applicants must appoint a US-licensed attorney to file a trademark application with the USPTO. LLCPad works with licensed trademark attorneys to ensure your application meets all legal requirements. The entire process typically takes 8–12 months from filing to registration.

services.name: EIN for Non-US Residents
services.shortDesc: Get your EIN (Tax ID) without an SSN. Required for US bank accounts, Amazon seller accounts & tax filing. Fast processing for international business owners.
services.metaTitle: Get an EIN Number Online — No SSN Required | LLCPad
services.metaDescription: Apply for a US EIN (Tax ID) without an SSN. Fast IRS processing for non-US residents. Required for banking, taxes & hiring. Start your application today.
services.description:
An EIN (Employer Identification Number) is a nine-digit tax identification number issued by the IRS for your US business. Every LLC needs an EIN to open a US bank account, file taxes, and hire employees. If you're a non-US resident without a Social Security Number, LLCPad handles the entire IRS application on your behalf — no SSN required.

Why You Need an EIN

  • US Bank Account: Banks require an EIN to open a business checking account — it's the first thing they ask for after your formation documents.
  • Tax Compliance: The IRS uses your EIN to track business tax filings, and payment processors like Stripe and PayPal require it for payouts.
  • Hire Contractors & Employees: You cannot issue W-9 forms or 1099s to US-based contractors without a valid EIN.
  • Business Credibility: An EIN separates your personal and business identities, which is essential for building credit and signing contracts.
Feature RecommendedEIN ITIN SSN
Who Needs It Businesses & LLCs Individuals (tax filing) US Citizens & Residents
Issued By IRS IRS SSA
Format XX-XXXXXXX 9XX-XX-XXXX XXX-XX-XXXX
Processing Time 1–4 weeks (by mail) 7–11 weeks Immediate (in person)
SSN Required ✗ No ✗ No N/A

How It Works

  1. Provide Your LLC Details: Share your LLC name, formation state, and responsible party information — we prepare IRS Form SS-4 on your behalf.
  2. We File with the IRS: Our team submits your EIN application directly to the IRS using the appropriate method for non-US applicants (fax or mail).
  3. Receive Your EIN: Once approved, you receive your official IRS EIN confirmation letter (CP 575) — ready to use for banking and tax filing.

Important: Non-US residents cannot apply for an EIN online through the IRS website. The IRS requires fax or mail submissions for foreign applicants, which typically takes 1–4 weeks for processing.

services.name: ITIN Application
services.shortDesc: Get your Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN) for US tax filing. Required for foreign nationals without SSN. CAA-assisted processing.
services.metaTitle: ITIN Application for Non-US Residents | LLCPad
services.metaDescription: Apply for a US ITIN (Individual Taxpayer ID) without visiting the US. Required for tax filing & treaty benefits. CAA-assisted processing for non-residents.
services.description:
An ITIN (Individual Taxpayer Identification Number) is a tax processing number issued by the IRS to individuals who are required to file US taxes but are not eligible for a Social Security Number. If you're a non-US resident who owns a US LLC, an ITIN allows you to file personal tax returns, claim tax treaty benefits, and meet withholding requirements.

Why You Need an ITIN

  • US Tax Filing: Non-resident LLC owners who earn US-sourced income must file a US tax return — and you need either an SSN or ITIN to do so.
  • Tax Treaty Benefits: An ITIN lets you claim reduced withholding rates under the US tax treaty with your home country (available for India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, and many others).
  • Avoid 30% Withholding: Without an ITIN, payment processors and banks may withhold 30% of your US income for backup withholding — an ITIN can reduce or eliminate this.
  • Build US Financial History: Some US financial institutions accept an ITIN for credit applications, helping you build a US financial footprint over time.
Feature RecommendedITIN EIN
Purpose Personal tax filing Business tax identification
Who Needs It Non-resident individuals Businesses & LLCs
Format 9XX-XX-XXXX XX-XXXXXXX
Processing Time 7–11 weeks 1–4 weeks
Required Documents Passport + W-7 form + tax return SS-4 form + LLC docs
Issued By IRS IRS

How It Works

  1. Gather Your Documents: You need a valid passport (or certified copies) and the completed IRS Form W-7 — our team prepares the W-7 for you based on your information.
  2. CAA-Assisted Processing: We work with IRS-authorized Certifying Acceptance Agents (CAAs) who can certify your passport copies, so you don't need to mail your original passport to the IRS.
  3. Receive Your ITIN: The IRS mails your ITIN assignment letter (CP 565) once processing is complete — typically 7 to 11 weeks after submission.

Important: An ITIN is for tax purposes only — it does not authorize work in the US or affect your immigration status. ITINs must be renewed if not used on a federal tax return for three consecutive years.

services.description:
A Certificate of Good Standing (also called a Certificate of Existence or Certificate of Status) is an official state document confirming that your LLC is properly registered, compliant with all filings, and authorized to do business. Banks, government agencies, and business partners frequently require this document.   • Tax Election Support: Your Operating Agreement documents how profits and losses are distributed, which is critical for proper tax filing.

  3. Review & Sign: Receive your completed Operating Agreement ready for signing — use it immediately for bank account applications and legal protection.

A reseller certificate (also called a sales tax permit or resale certificate) allows your LLC to purchase goods for resale without paying sales tax to the supplier. Instead, you collect sales tax from the end customer and remit it to the state. For international entrepreneurs selling physical products through Amazon, Shopify, or other US channels, a reseller certificate is essential for legal tax compliance and maximizing profit margins.

Why You Need a Reseller Certificate

  • Buy Inventory Tax-Free: Without a reseller certificate, you pay sales tax on every wholesale purchase — an unnecessary cost that cuts into your margins when you resell those products.
  • Legal Sales Tax Compliance: If your LLC has sales tax nexus in a state (physical or economic), you're legally required to collect and remit sales tax. A reseller certificate is the foundation of this compliance.
  • Amazon & Marketplace Requirements: Amazon and other marketplaces may request your reseller certificate or sales tax permit number during seller registration or tax configuration.
  • Avoid Audit Penalties: States actively audit businesses for sales tax compliance. Operating without proper permits can result in back taxes, penalties of 10–25%, and interest charges.

How It Works

  1. Identify Your Nexus States: We help you determine which states require you to register based on your sales volume, warehouse locations, or marketplace activity.
  2. We Register Your LLC: Our team completes the state sales tax registration application and obtains your reseller certificate / sales tax permit on your behalf.
  3. Start Buying Tax-Free: Use your reseller certificate with suppliers to purchase inventory without paying sales tax, and begin collecting tax from your customers as required.

Important: Economic nexus thresholds are based on the previous 12 months of sales into a state. If you sell on Amazon FBA, your inventory stored in Amazon warehouses creates physical nexus in those states regardless of sales volume. services.metaTitle: Apostille Service for US LLC Documents | LLCPad
services.metaDescription: Get apostille certification for US LLC documents. Use your formation papers internationally. LLCPad apostille service for non-US entrepreneurs.
services.description:
An apostille is an international certification that authenticates US documents for use in foreign countries under the Hague Apostille Convention. If you need to use your LLC formation documents, certificates, or legal paperwork in Bangladesh, India, UAE, Pakistan, or any other Hague member country, an apostille is required. LLCPad handles the complete apostille process — from document preparation to authentication by the US Secretary of State.

Why You Need Apostille Service

  • International Business Use: Foreign banks, government agencies, and business partners require apostilled documents to verify the legitimacy of your US LLC.
  • Open Foreign Bank Accounts: If you're opening a business bank account in your home country using your US LLC, the bank will typically require apostilled formation documents.
  • Legal Proceedings Abroad: Court proceedings, property transactions, or regulatory filings in other countries require US documents to be apostilled for legal validity.
  • Government Registrations: Many countries require apostilled documents when registering a foreign-owned company, applying for trade licenses, or filing tax registrations.
Document Common Use Case Processing Time Notarization Needed
Certificate of Formation Foreign bank accounts, trade licenses 5 – 10 business days ✗ No (state-issued)
Certificate of Good Standing Annual bank verification, contracts 5 – 10 business days ✗ No (state-issued)
Operating Agreement Proof of ownership, partner disputes 7 – 15 business days ✓ Yes (notarize first)
EIN Confirmation Letter Tax registration in home country 7 – 15 business days ✓ Yes (notarize first)
Power of Attorney Authorize representatives abroad 7 – 15 business days ✓ Yes (notarize first)

How It Works

  1. Send Us Your Documents: Upload or mail the documents you need apostilled. We'll review them and advise if notarization is required before apostille.
  2. We Notarize & Apostille: If needed, we arrange notarization through a licensed US notary, then submit the documents to the appropriate Secretary of State for apostille certification.
  3. Receive Apostilled Documents: Your authenticated documents are shipped to your international address via express courier with tracking, or delivered as certified digital copies where accepted.

Important: Apostilles only work for countries that are part of the Hague Apostille Convention. For non-Hague countries, documents require embassy legalization instead (a longer process). Also note that federal documents (like IRS letters) must be apostilled by the US Department of State in Washington, DC — not the state Secretary of State.

faqs.question: How long does apostille processing take?
faqs.answer: Standard processing takes 5-10 business days depending on the state. Rush processing (2-3 days) is available for an additional fee. Some states are faster than others.

faqs.question: Does my country accept apostilles?
faqs.answer: Most countries accept apostilles, including all EU countries, UK, India, UAE, and many others. However, some countries (like China and Canada) are not part of the Hague Convention and require different authentication (embassy legalization).

faqs.question: Can any document be apostilled?
faqs.answer: Only official documents or notarized documents can be apostilled. Private documents (like contracts or agreements) must first be notarized before they can receive an apostille.

services.name: BOI Ownership Filing

services.metaTitle: BOI Report Filing — FinCEN Compliance | LLCPad
services.metaDescription: File your Beneficial Ownership Information (BOI) report with FinCEN. Required for all US LLCs. Avoid $500/day penalties. We handle it for non-US owners.
services.description:
The Beneficial Ownership Information (BOI) report is a federal filing required by FinCEN (Financial Crimes Enforcement Network) under the Corporate Transparency Act. All US LLCs must report their beneficial owners — the individuals who own or control the company. Non-compliance carries severe penalties, making this a critical filing for every non-US LLC owner.

Why You Need to File a BOI Report

  • Federal Law Requirement: The Corporate Transparency Act requires most LLCs and corporations to file a BOI report with FinCEN — this is not optional.
  • Avoid Heavy Penalties: Failure to file or filing false information can result in civil penalties of up to $500 per day and criminal penalties of up to $10,000 and 2 years imprisonment.
  • Applies to Non-US Owners: If you are a non-US resident who owns or controls a US LLC, you must be reported as a beneficial owner regardless of your country of residence.
  • Quick & One-Time Filing: For most small LLCs, this is a straightforward one-time report — but you must update it within 30 days if ownership changes.
Requirement LLC Corporation Exempt Entities
BOI Filing Required ✓ Yes ✓ Yes ✗ No (23 exemptions)
Deadline (New Entities) 90 days after formation 90 days after formation N/A
Deadline (Existing Entities) Jan 1, 2025 Jan 1, 2025 N/A
Penalty (Non-Filing) $500/day $500/day N/A
Info Required Name, DOB, address, ID Name, DOB, address, ID N/A

How It Works

  1. Provide Owner Details: Share the name, date of birth, residential address, and a copy of a government-issued ID for each beneficial owner (anyone owning 25%+ or exercising substantial control).
  2. We Prepare & File: Our team completes the BOI report and submits it electronically to FinCEN through their secure Beneficial Ownership Secure System (BOSS).
  3. Receive Confirmation: You get a filing confirmation with your FinCEN ID — keep this for your records and let us know within 30 days if any ownership details change.

Important: BOI information is not public — it is stored in a secure FinCEN database accessible only to authorized government agencies and financial institutions. You must update your BOI report within 30 days of any change in beneficial ownership or company information.

services.metaDescription: File Form 5472 for your foreign-owned US LLC. Avoid the $25,000 IRS penalty. Expert preparation for non-US residents. Get started now.
services.description:
If you are a non-US resident who owns a single-member LLC, the IRS requires you to file Form 5472 along with a pro-forma Form 1120 every year. This is an information return — not an income tax return — but the penalty for failing to file is a staggering $25,000 per form, per year. Our service ensures your Form 5472 is prepared accurately and filed on time.

Why You Need Form 5472 Filing

  • Mandatory for Foreign Owners: Every foreign-owned disregarded entity (single-member LLC) must report transactions between the LLC and its foreign owner — including capital contributions, loans, and distributions.
  • $25,000 Penalty per Violation: The IRS imposes one of its harshest penalties on this form. There is no reduced penalty for small businesses or first-time filers.
  • Required Even with No Revenue: Transferring money into your LLC bank account, paying for a registered agent, or covering any expense counts as a reportable transaction.
  • Protect Your US Presence: Consistent Form 5472 compliance keeps your LLC, EIN, and bank accounts in good standing with federal authorities.
Requirement Details
Who Must File Any US LLC with 25%+ foreign ownership that has reportable transactions
Filed Alongside Pro-forma Form 1120 (even though disregarded for tax purposes)
Filing Deadline April 15 (with extension available to October 15)
Late Filing Penalty $25,000 per form per year
What Is Reported Capital contributions, loans, distributions, payments for services, and any monetary transactions between the LLC and its foreign owner
Exemptions Only if no reportable transactions occurred (rare — even initial funding counts)
Filing Method Paper filing only (IRS does not accept e-filing for 5472 with pro-forma 1120)

How It Works

  1. Provide Transaction Records: Share details of all transactions between you (the foreign owner) and your LLC — bank transfers, expense payments, capital contributions, and distributions.
  2. We Prepare Form 5472 + 1120: Our team completes both forms with all required schedules, ensuring every reportable transaction is accurately documented.
  3. Filing and Confirmation: Since Form 5472 with a pro-forma 1120 must be paper-filed, we prepare your complete mailing package with the correct IRS service center address and provide tracking confirmation.

Important: The $25,000 penalty applies per form, per year. If you have missed previous years, each unfiled year carries its own $25,000 penalty. We can help you file delinquent returns with reasonable cause statements to request penalty abatement where applicable.

services.name: S-Corp Election
services.shortDesc: File IRS Form 2553 to elect S-Corporation tax status for your LLC. Reduce self-employment taxes and save thousands annually.
services.metaTitle: S-Corp Election (Form 2553) for LLCs | LLCPad
services.metaDescription: Elect S-Corp status for your US LLC and reduce self-employment tax. Form 2553 filing for business owners. See if you qualify — start now.
services.description:
An S-Corp Election allows your LLC to be taxed as an S-Corporation, which can result in significant tax savings once your business generates consistent revenue. By filing Form 2553 with the IRS, you split your income into a reasonable salary (subject to payroll tax) and distributions (which are not), potentially saving thousands annually in self-employment tax.

Why You Need S-Corp Election

  • Reduce Self-Employment Tax: As a default LLC, all net income is subject to 15.3% self-employment tax. With an S-Corp, only your salary portion is taxed — distributions are exempt.
  • Save Thousands Annually: For LLCs earning above $50,000–$60,000 in net profit, the payroll tax savings often outweigh the additional compliance costs of S-Corp status.
  • Maintain LLC Legal Protection: Your LLC structure stays intact — S-Corp is purely a tax classification change with the IRS, not a change to your legal entity.
  • Professional Tax Planning: We evaluate whether S-Corp election makes financial sense for your specific situation before filing, ensuring you only elect when it benefits you.
Factor Default LLC RecommendedLLC with S-Corp Election
Self-Employment Tax (15.3%) On all net income Only on salary portion
Salary Requirement None Must pay reasonable salary
Tax Filing Complexity Simple (Schedule C or 1065) Moderate (Form 1120-S + payroll)
Best for Revenue Above Any amount $50,000+ net profit/year
Annual Compliance Cost Lower Higher (payroll + 1120-S filing)

How It Works

  1. Eligibility Review: We verify your LLC qualifies for S-Corp election — including shareholder limits, residency requirements, and entity structure. Note that non-resident aliens generally cannot be S-Corp shareholders, so this service is primarily for US residents or those with ITIN/SSN status.
  2. Form 2553 Preparation: We complete and file Form 2553 with the IRS. To be effective for the current tax year, it must be filed within 75 days of formation or by March 15 of the tax year.
  3. Payroll Setup Guidance: Once approved, we guide you on setting up reasonable salary payments and quarterly payroll tax filings to maintain compliance.

Important: S-Corp election is not beneficial for every LLC. If your net income is below $50,000 annually, the additional payroll and filing costs may exceed the tax savings. Non-resident aliens without an SSN or ITIN are generally ineligible for S-Corp status. We recommend a consultation before proceeding.

faqs.question: Can I elect S-Corp if I'm a nonresident alien living outside the US?
faqs.answer: No. The IRS strictly prohibits nonresident aliens from being S-Corporation shareholders. This service is only available to US citizens, green card holders, or resident aliens meeting the Substantial Presence Test.

faqs.question: When does S-Corp election become beneficial?
faqs.answer: Generally when your LLC's net income exceeds $40,000-$50,000 annually. Below that threshold, the administrative costs of running payroll and additional tax filings may outweigh the savings. We recommend consulting with a tax advisor for your specific situation.

faqs.question: What changes after the IRS approves my S-Corp election?
faqs.answer: You'll receive a CP261 notice confirming acceptance. Going forward, you must file Form 1120-S annually, run payroll for owner-employees, pay reasonable salaries, and issue W-2s and K-1s. The LLC continues operating the same way legally.

services.description:
State sales tax registration is required whenever your LLC has nexus — a taxable presence — in a US state. Whether you sell physical products, digital goods, or certain services, you must register with each state where you meet the nexus threshold, collect the correct sales tax rate, and file periodic returns. LLCPad simplifies multi-state sales tax registration for international business owners who sell into the US market.

Why You Need Sales Tax Registration

  • Legal Requirement: Selling into a state where you have nexus without collecting sales tax is illegal. States aggressively enforce compliance through audits, penalties, and interest on unpaid tax.
  • Marketplace Compliance: While Amazon and other marketplaces collect tax in most states, some states still require sellers to register and file returns — even if the marketplace handles collection.
  • Avoid Back-Tax Liability: If a state audits your LLC and discovers unregistered sales, you may owe back taxes for the entire period plus penalties of 10–30% and accrued interest.
  • Enable Resale Purchasing: Sales tax registration is a prerequisite for obtaining a reseller certificate, which allows you to buy wholesale inventory tax-free.

services.name: Stripe / PayPal Setup
services.shortDesc: Set up Stripe and PayPal business accounts for your US LLC. Complete verification, bank linking, and payment integration ready.
services.metaTitle: Stripe & PayPal Setup for US LLCs | LLCPad
services.metaDescription: Set up Stripe and PayPal for your US LLC as a non-resident. Accept global payments with your EIN. Expert setup assistance — get started.
services.description:
Accepting payments is the lifeblood of any online business. With a properly formed US LLC and EIN, you can set up Stripe, PayPal, and other major payment processors to accept credit cards, debit cards, and digital payments from customers worldwide. Our setup service ensures your accounts are configured correctly from day one — avoiding the common verification issues and holds that non-resident LLC owners frequently encounter.

Why You Need Professional Setup

  • EIN Required: US payment processors require a valid EIN (Employer Identification Number) to open a business account. We verify your EIN is properly formatted and linked to your LLC before starting.
  • Avoid Account Freezes: Incorrect business details, mismatched documents, or incomplete verification are the top reasons payment accounts get frozen. Professional setup prevents these issues.
  • Accept Global Payments: Stripe alone supports 135+ currencies and all major card networks. Combined with PayPal, you can serve customers virtually anywhere in the world.
  • Faster Payouts: Properly configured accounts with verified banking details receive payouts on standard schedules without the delays that flag new or unverified accounts.
Feature RecommendedStripe PayPal Wise Square
Transaction Fee 2.9% + $0.30 3.49% + $0.49 0.5%–2% (varies by currency) 2.6% + $0.10
International Support 135+ currencies, 46+ countries 200+ markets, 25 currencies 40+ currencies, best exchange rates US, Canada, UK, Australia, Japan
Payout Speed 2 business days (instant available) 1–3 business days 1–2 business days 1–2 business days
Integration Ease Best — extensive API and plugins Good — widely supported Limited — primarily for transfers Good — POS + online
Best For SaaS, e-commerce, online services Marketplaces, invoicing, buyer trust International freelancers, B2B payments In-person + online retail

How It Works

  1. Pre-Setup Verification: We verify your LLC documents, EIN confirmation letter, and US bank account are ready. You need all three before applying for Stripe or PayPal business accounts.
  2. Account Creation and Verification: We guide you through each step of the signup process — entering business details, uploading documents, and completing identity verification to avoid triggering account reviews.
  3. Configuration and Testing: Once approved, we help configure payout schedules, connect your US bank account, set up webhook notifications, and run test transactions to confirm everything works.

Important: Stripe requires a valid EIN and a US bank account — a personal bank account or non-US account will not work. PayPal Business may request additional verification for non-resident owners. We recommend setting up both processors for redundancy, as having a backup prevents revenue loss if one account is temporarily under review.

faqs.question: Can I open US Stripe/PayPal without an SSN?
faqs.answer: For Stripe, you can often complete setup with your EIN and passport. For PayPal, an ITIN is strongly recommended — PayPal needs to verify both the business (EIN) and the individual owner (SSN or ITIN). Without an ITIN, your PayPal account may have limited functionality.

faqs.question: Do I need a US bank account?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both Stripe and PayPal require a US bank account in your LLC's name for payouts. Several online banks (Mercury, Relay, Novo) allow non-resident LLC owners to open accounts remotely. We can recommend the best option.

faqs.question: What are the transaction fees?
faqs.answer: Stripe charges 2.9% + $0.30 per successful card transaction. PayPal charges 2.99% + $0.49 per transaction. Both have additional fees for international cards and currency conversion. Our $99 setup fee covers account creation only — transaction fees are paid directly to Stripe/PayPal.

services.name: Financial Report Preparation
services.shortDesc: Professional financial statements for your US LLC. Income statements, balance sheets, and cash flow reports for banking, tax, and investor needs.
services.metaTitle: Financial Reports for US LLCs | LLCPad
services.metaDescription: Get professional financial statements for your US LLC. Balance sheets, income statements, and cash flow reports. Essential for tax and banking.
services.description:
Professional financial reports give you a clear picture of your LLC's health and are often required by banks, investors, the IRS, and payment processors. Our Financial Report service prepares accurate statements from your bookkeeping data — including balance sheets, income statements, cash flow reports, and tax summaries — so you can make informed decisions and meet compliance requirements with confidence.

Why You Need Financial Reports

  • Bank Account Requirements: US banks like Mercury and Relay may request financial statements during account reviews or when you apply for credit products.
  • Tax Filing Support: Accurate financial reports make tax preparation faster and more accurate, reducing the risk of errors on your Form 5472, 1120, or 1065 filings.
  • Business Performance Insight: Understand your revenue trends, expense breakdown, profit margins, and cash position without guessing — decisions backed by real numbers.
  • Investor and Partner Confidence: If you seek funding or bring on partners, professional financial statements demonstrate operational maturity and transparency.
Report Type Purpose Who Needs It
Balance Sheet Shows assets, liabilities, and owner equity at a specific point in time Banks, investors, loan applications
Income Statement (P&L) Shows revenue, expenses, and net profit over a period Business owners, tax preparers, partners
Cash Flow Statement Tracks cash inflows and outflows from operations, investing, and financing Business owners managing liquidity
Tax Summary Report Consolidates deductible expenses, income categories, and figures needed for tax returns Tax preparers, CPA firms, IRS filing

How It Works

  1. Provide Your Financial Data: Share your bookkeeping records, bank statements, and transaction history. If you use our Bookkeeping service, we already have everything needed.
  2. We Prepare Your Reports: Our team compiles and reviews your data, then generates professional financial statements following US accounting standards.
  3. Delivery and Consultation: Receive your reports in PDF and spreadsheet formats. We walk you through key numbers and flag anything that needs attention before tax season.

Important: Financial reports are only as accurate as the underlying bookkeeping data. We recommend maintaining consistent monthly or quarterly bookkeeping to ensure your year-end financial statements are reliable and audit-ready.

comparisonFeatures.description: Detailed Profit & Loss statement showing revenue, costs, and net income.
comparisonFeatures.tooltip: Income statements reveal your business profitability over a specific period.

comparisonFeatures.tooltip: Accurate categorization ensures meaningful financial analysis and tax optimization.

comparisonFeatures.tooltip: Comparing periods reveals growth patterns, seasonal trends, and areas for improvement.

faqs.question: What types of financial reports will I receive?
faqs.answer: The standard package includes three core reports: Income Statement (Profit & Loss), Balance Sheet, and Cash Flow Statement. Additional reports like accounts receivable aging, budget vs. actual, and key metrics dashboards are available in the Annual package or upon request.

faqs.question: Do I need accounting software?
faqs.answer: While not required, using software like QuickBooks or Xero makes the process faster and less expensive. If you're tracking everything in spreadsheets or bank statements, we can work with that. We strongly recommend migrating to proper accounting software and can help with setup.

faqs.question: How often should I get financial reports?
faqs.answer: For most small LLCs, quarterly reports are the minimum recommendation. Monthly reports are ideal for active cash flow management or if you're seeking funding. Annual reports are needed at minimum for tax preparation.

blogs.title: Wyoming vs Delaware LLC for Non-Residents: Which State Should You Choose in 2026?
blogs.excerpt: Privacy, fees, annual costs, and tax implications — we break down the real differences that matter when forming a US LLC from abroad.
blogs.metaTitle: Wyoming vs Delaware LLC for Non-Residents (2026)
blogs.metaDescription: Compare Wyoming vs Delaware LLC for non-residents. Privacy, fees, taxes, annual costs — find the best state for your foreign-owned LLC in 2026.
blogs.content:
If you are forming a US LLC from outside the United States, you have probably seen the same two states recommended everywhere: Wyoming and Delaware. Both are genuinely excellent choices for non-resident LLC owners, but they are not interchangeable. The right pick depends on your business type, your budget, and how much you value privacy.

In this guide, we will walk through the real, practical differences between Wyoming and Delaware LLCs in 2026 — not the recycled marketing copy you find on most formation sites. By the end, you will know exactly which state fits your situation.

Quick Comparison: Wyoming vs Delaware LLC

Before we dive into the details, here is a side-by-side snapshot of the key differences that matter most to non-resident LLC owners.

FeatureWyomingDelaware
Formation Fee$100$90
Annual Report / Franchise Tax$60/year (minimum)$300/year flat tax
State Income TaxNoneNone (for out-of-state activity)
Privacy (Public Records)Members/managers not listedMembers/managers not listed
Registered Agent RequiredYesYes
Court System ReputationStandardCourt of Chancery (gold standard)
Asset ProtectionStrongest in the USStrong
BOI Reporting (FinCEN)Required (federal)Required (federal)
Best ForSmall businesses, e-commerce, freelancersInvestor-funded startups, holding companies

Formation Costs and Annual Fees

This is where the rubber meets the road for most non-resident founders. When you are running an online business from Bangladesh, India, Pakistan, or the UAE, every dollar counts — especially in the early stages.

Wyoming: The Budget-Friendly Option

Wyoming charges a $100 formation fee for LLCs. Your annual report costs a minimum of $60 per year, calculated based on your assets in Wyoming. If you run a service business or an online store with no physical assets in the state (which describes most non-resident LLCs), you will pay that $60 minimum.

Total first-year cost with the state: roughly $100. Total ongoing cost: $60 per year. That is genuinely hard to beat.

Delaware: The Premium Choice

Delaware's formation fee is slightly lower at $90, but the annual franchise tax for LLCs is a flat $300 per year — no exceptions, no reductions for small businesses. Whether you earn $0 or $10 million, you pay $300.

Over five years, that is a difference of $1,200 in state fees alone ($300 vs $1,500 for Delaware versus $300 vs $500 for Wyoming). Add in registered agent fees, and the gap becomes meaningful for bootstrapped founders.

Privacy Protections

Privacy is often the number-one concern for non-resident LLC owners, and both states deliver well here — but Wyoming has a slight edge.

Wyoming Privacy

  • LLC members and managers are not listed in public formation documents
  • Only the registered agent's name and address appear on the Articles of Organization
  • Wyoming was the first state to create the LLC structure (1977) and has consistently strengthened privacy protections
  • Nominee services can add another layer if needed, though most non-residents do not require this

Delaware Privacy

  • Members and managers are also not listed publicly
  • The Certificate of Formation only requires the LLC name and registered agent
  • Delaware's privacy is functionally equivalent to Wyoming's for most purposes

One important note for 2026: Beneficial Ownership Information (BOI) reporting is a federal requirement that applies regardless of which state you choose. All LLCs must report their beneficial owners to FinCEN. This does not change the state-level privacy picture, but it means complete anonymity is no longer possible at the federal level.

Tax Implications for Foreign Owners

Here is some good news: neither Wyoming nor Delaware imposes a state-level income tax on LLC income earned outside the state. For non-residents running online businesses, this means zero state income tax in either case.

However, you still have federal obligations:

  • Form 5472 + Pro Forma 1120: Every foreign-owned single-member LLC must file this annually with the IRS, even if you earned zero income. The penalty for not filing is $25,000 per form. Read our complete Form 5472 guide to understand your obligations.
  • EIN (Employer Identification Number): Required for tax filing, banking, and most business activities. You can apply for an EIN through LLCPad — the process takes about 4-6 weeks for non-residents with no SSN.
  • ITIN: Some foreign owners need an Individual Taxpayer Identification Number, though this depends on your specific situation and whether you have US-source income.

The bottom line on taxes: your state choice does not significantly change your federal tax obligations. Both Wyoming and Delaware are neutral in this regard.

Asset Protection

If asset protection matters to you — and it should if you are building anything of value — Wyoming is the clear winner.

Wyoming offers what is called a charging order protection that is among the strongest in the country. In plain English, this means that if someone sues you personally, they cannot easily seize your LLC's assets. A creditor can only obtain a "charging order" against your LLC interest, which is essentially a lien on distributions — not ownership.

Delaware also offers charging order protection, but Wyoming's statutes are considered more protective, particularly for single-member LLCs. Some legal scholars argue that Delaware's protection for single-member LLCs is weaker because of certain court precedents.

The Delaware Court of Chancery Advantage

Delaware has one genuine, significant advantage: its Court of Chancery. This is a specialized business court with over 200 years of case law. Judges are experts in corporate and LLC disputes, and decisions tend to be more predictable and sophisticated.

This matters if:

  • You are raising venture capital or outside investment
  • Your LLC has multiple members with complex operating agreements
  • You anticipate business disputes that could end up in court
  • You plan to convert to a corporation later for an IPO or acquisition

For a solo founder running an e-commerce store or freelance business? The Court of Chancery is unlikely to be relevant. This advantage is real but niche.

Which State Is Best for Your Business Type?

E-Commerce and Amazon Sellers

Recommendation: Wyoming. You need low costs, strong privacy, and straightforward compliance. Wyoming delivers all three. Most Amazon seller accounts, Shopify stores, and e-commerce businesses do not need Delaware's court system or investor-friendly reputation. Start your Wyoming LLC formation here.

Freelancers and Service Businesses

Recommendation: Wyoming. If you are a consultant, developer, designer, or any kind of service provider billing international clients through a US LLC, Wyoming's $60/year annual fee and zero state income tax make it the obvious choice.

Startups Seeking Investment

Recommendation: Delaware. If you plan to raise money from US-based investors, they will often expect (or require) a Delaware entity. Venture capital term sheets, SAFE notes, and convertible notes are typically drafted with Delaware law in mind. The $300/year premium is worth it for the credibility and legal infrastructure.

Holding Companies

Recommendation: Wyoming or Delaware — depends on complexity. For simple holding structures (holding IP, real estate, or other LLCs), Wyoming's low fees win. For complex multi-entity structures with potential investor involvement, Delaware's legal precedents provide more certainty.

Annual Filing Requirements

Staying compliant is just as important as choosing the right state. Here is what you need to file each year:

Wyoming Annual Requirements

  1. Annual Report: Due on the first day of the month your LLC was formed. Fee: $60 minimum (based on Wyoming assets).
  2. Registered Agent: Must maintain a Wyoming registered agent at all times.
  3. Federal Filings: Form 5472 + Pro Forma 1120 (due April 15, can extend to October 15).
  4. BOI Report: File with FinCEN within 90 days of formation (existing LLCs had a January 1, 2025 deadline, now subject to ongoing updates).

Delaware Annual Requirements

  1. Annual Franchise Tax: Due June 1 each year. Fee: $300 flat.
  2. Registered Agent: Must maintain a Delaware registered agent at all times.
  3. Federal Filings: Same as Wyoming — Form 5472 + Pro Forma 1120.
  4. BOI Report: Same federal requirement as Wyoming.

Missing your annual report or franchise tax can result in your LLC being administratively dissolved. LLCPad offers registered agent services that include compliance reminders so you never miss a deadline.

BOI Reporting in 2026: What You Need to Know

The Beneficial Ownership Information (BOI) reporting requirement under the Corporate Transparency Act applies to both Wyoming and Delaware LLCs equally. As a non-resident owner, you must report:

  • Your full legal name
  • Date of birth
  • Current residential address
  • A unique identifying number from a passport or government-issued ID

This information goes to FinCEN (the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network) and is not publicly accessible. It is shared only with law enforcement and certain financial institutions. The requirement exists regardless of which state your LLC is formed in.

Our Recommendation for Most Non-Residents

For the majority of non-resident entrepreneurs — especially those running e-commerce businesses, Amazon stores, freelance practices, or online service companies — Wyoming is the better choice. Here is why:

  • 80% lower annual costs ($60 vs $300)
  • Stronger asset protection for single-member LLCs
  • Equivalent privacy at the state level
  • Same federal tax obligations regardless of state
  • Simpler compliance with fewer gotchas

Choose Delaware only if you have a specific, concrete reason: raising VC funding, setting up a complex multi-member structure, or an investor specifically requiring it.

How LLCPad Makes It Easy

Whether you choose Wyoming or Delaware, LLCPad handles the entire formation process for non-residents:

  • State filing: We prepare and file your Articles of Organization
  • Registered agent: Included in your formation package for the first year
  • EIN application: We obtain your Employer Identification Number from the IRS
  • Operating Agreement: Custom-drafted for your business structure
  • Compliance calendar: Automated reminders for annual filings and deadlines

Ready to get started? Form your LLC today and we will guide you through every step — from state selection to your first bank account.

Disclaimer: LLCPad is not a law firm and does not provide legal or tax advice. This guide is for informational purposes only. Consult a qualified attorney or CPA for advice specific to your situation.

blogs.title: Form 5472: The $25,000 Filing Every Foreign LLC Owner Must Know
blogs.excerpt: Even with zero income, this IRS filing is mandatory for foreign-owned LLCs. Miss it, and you face $25,000 in penalties per form.
blogs.metaTitle: Form 5472 Guide for Foreign LLC Owners (2026)
blogs.metaDescription: Complete guide to IRS Form 5472 for foreign-owned LLCs. Filing requirements, $25,000 penalty, deadlines, and step-by-step instructions.
blogs.content:
If you own a US LLC as a non-resident, there is one IRS filing that you absolutely cannot afford to ignore: Form 5472. Even if your LLC earned zero revenue last year, even if you have not started doing business yet, this filing is almost certainly required — and the penalty for skipping it is $25,000 per form, per year.

That is not a typo. Twenty-five thousand dollars. For a form that many foreign LLC owners have never heard of until it is too late.

This guide will explain exactly what Form 5472 is, who needs to file, what information is required, and how to avoid the most common mistakes that trigger IRS penalties.

What Is Form 5472?

Form 5472, officially titled "Information Return of a 25% Foreign-Owned U.S. Corporation or a Foreign Corporation Engaged in a U.S. Trade or Business," is an information return filed with the IRS. It reports transactions between a US entity and its foreign owners or related foreign parties.

For our purposes, here is what matters: if you are a non-US person who owns a single-member LLC in the United States, the IRS treats your LLC as a "reporting corporation" and requires you to file Form 5472 along with a pro forma (placeholder) Form 1120.

This requirement was expanded significantly by Treasury Regulations finalized in 2017. Before that, single-member LLCs owned by foreigners were largely invisible to the IRS. Now, they are firmly on the radar.

Who Must File Form 5472?

You must file Form 5472 if all of the following are true:

  1. You are a non-US person (not a US citizen, green card holder, or tax resident)
  2. You own 25% or more of a US LLC (if you are the sole owner, you own 100%)
  3. The LLC had reportable transactions during the tax year (this is broader than you might think — see below)

In practice, this covers virtually every foreign-owned single-member LLC, because the definition of "reportable transactions" is extremely broad.

What Counts as a Reportable Transaction?

This is where most people get caught off guard. "Reportable transactions" does not just mean revenue or sales. The IRS defines reportable transactions to include:

  • Capital contributions: Any money you transfer into your LLC's bank account, including the initial deposit to open the account
  • Distributions: Any money you withdraw from the LLC for personal use
  • Loans: Any loans between you and the LLC, in either direction
  • Payments for services: If you provide services to the LLC or the LLC pays you
  • Rent and lease payments: If the LLC pays you rent or you provide property to the LLC
  • Sales of goods or property: Between you and the LLC
  • Commissions and fees: Paid between you and the LLC
  • Insurance premiums: Paid between related parties

Here is the critical point: if you deposited even $1 into your LLC's bank account, you have a reportable transaction. If you used personal funds to pay for the LLC's registered agent, domain name, or any other business expense, that is a reportable transaction.

This means that even a completely dormant LLC — one that has never earned a penny — typically has reportable transactions and must file Form 5472.

Form 5472 Filing Requirements: Step by Step

Step 1: Gather Your Information

Before you start filling out Form 5472, collect the following:

  • LLC details: Legal name, EIN (Employer Identification Number), address, state of formation, date of incorporation
  • Owner details: Your full legal name, country of citizenship, foreign taxpayer identification number (if any), address
  • Transaction records: A complete record of every transaction between you and the LLC during the tax year (January 1 through December 31)

If you do not have an EIN yet, you must obtain one before filing. LLCPad can help you get your EIN — the process typically takes 4-6 weeks for non-residents.

Step 2: Complete the Pro Forma Form 1120

Foreign-owned single-member LLCs file Form 5472 attached to a pro forma Form 1120 (U.S. Corporation Income Tax Return). "Pro forma" means it is a placeholder — you are not actually filing as a corporation.

On the Form 1120, you will:

  • Write the LLC's name, EIN, and address at the top
  • Check the box indicating this is a pro forma return
  • Enter zeros on all income and deduction lines (unless advised otherwise by your CPA)
  • Attach Form 5472 as a required schedule

Step 3: Fill Out Form 5472 Itself

Form 5472 has several parts:

  • Part I — Reporting Corporation: Your LLC's information (name, EIN, address, date and country of incorporation, total assets)
  • Part II — 25% Foreign Shareholder: Your personal information as the foreign owner (name, address, country of citizenship, taxpayer ID)
  • Part III — Related Party: Usually the same as Part II if you are the sole owner
  • Part IV — Monetary Transactions: This is the core of the form. You report the dollar amounts of each category of transaction between you and the LLC. Common entries include capital contributions (line 14), distributions (line 20), and other transactions.
  • Part V — Reportable Transactions of a Foreign-Owned U.S. DE: This section is specifically for disregarded entities (single-member LLCs). Report the total amounts by category.
  • Part VI — Additional Information: Various questions about the nature of the reporting corporation and its activities.

Step 4: File by the Deadline

The filing deadline for Form 5472 with the pro forma 1120 is April 15 of the year following the tax year. For the 2025 tax year, the deadline is April 15, 2026.

You can request an automatic 6-month extension by filing Form 7004 before the April 15 deadline. This extends your filing deadline to October 15. The extension is for the filing only — it does not extend the time to pay any taxes owed (though most foreign-owned single-member LLCs owe no income tax).

Step 5: Mail the Return

Unlike most IRS forms, the pro forma 1120 with Form 5472 cannot be e-filed. You must mail a paper return to:

Department of the Treasury
Internal Revenue Service Center
Ogden, UT 84201-0012

Use a trackable mailing service (USPS Certified Mail, FedEx, or UPS) so you have proof of timely filing. Keep copies of everything you send.

The $25,000 Penalty: What Happens If You Do Not File

The penalty for failing to file Form 5472, or filing one that is substantially incomplete, is $25,000 per form, per year. If the IRS sends you a notice and you still do not file within 90 days, an additional $25,000 penalty is assessed for each 30-day period of continued noncompliance.

This can add up horrifyingly fast. Two years of non-filing could result in $50,000 or more in penalties — for a form that might report nothing more than a $500 capital contribution.

The IRS has increased enforcement of international information returns in recent years. Automated systems now flag missing Form 5472 filings, and penalty notices are being issued more aggressively.

Can You Get the Penalty Abated?

Yes, in some cases. The IRS may abate (cancel) the penalty if you can demonstrate reasonable cause — meaning you had a legitimate reason for the failure and it was not due to willful neglect. Common reasonable cause arguments include:

  • You relied on a tax professional who gave you incorrect advice
  • You were unaware of the filing requirement despite acting in good faith
  • Circumstances beyond your control prevented timely filing (illness, natural disaster, etc.)

However, abatement is not guaranteed, and the process can take months. It is far better to file correctly and on time.

Common Mistakes That Trigger Penalties

Over the years, we have seen the same errors come up repeatedly among foreign LLC owners:

Mistake 1: Not Knowing the Form Exists

This is by far the most common problem. Many formation companies will set up your LLC and get your EIN but never mention Form 5472. You discover the requirement two or three years later, with $50,000-$75,000 in potential penalties hanging over your head.

Mistake 2: Assuming a Dormant LLC Does Not Need to File

As we explained above, even transferring money into the LLC's bank account creates a reportable transaction. A dormant LLC still needs to file Form 5472 in almost every case.

Mistake 3: Trying to E-File

The pro forma 1120 with Form 5472 must be paper-filed. Attempting to e-file will result in a rejection, and if you do not follow up with a paper filing, you will miss the deadline.

Mistake 4: Incomplete Transaction Reporting

Forgetting to include certain categories of transactions — like capital contributions or payments for the LLC's expenses from personal funds — can make your filing "substantially incomplete," which triggers the same $25,000 penalty as not filing at all.

Mistake 5: Wrong EIN or Name

Simple data entry errors — a transposed digit in the EIN, or the LLC name not matching IRS records exactly — can cause processing issues and potential penalty notices.

Record-Keeping Requirements

The IRS requires you to maintain records of all reportable transactions for at least 7 years from the date of filing. These records should include:

  • Bank statements showing all deposits and withdrawals
  • Records of any transfers between your personal accounts and the LLC
  • Invoices and receipts for business expenses
  • Loan agreements between you and the LLC
  • Documentation of any property transfers

Good record-keeping is your best defense if the IRS ever questions your filing. Use a separate bank account for your LLC — read our guide to opening a US bank account as a non-resident — and keep personal and business finances completely separate.

Do You Need a CPA to File Form 5472?

Technically, no — you can fill out the form yourself. But practically, we strongly recommend working with a tax professional who has experience with international filings. Here is why:

  • The penalties for errors are disproportionately high ($25,000 for a mistake on a form that might report $500 in transactions)
  • The interaction between Form 5472, the pro forma 1120, and other potential filings (FBAR, Form 8858, etc.) can be complex
  • A CPA's signature on the return provides some protection under the "reasonable cause" defense if issues arise
  • The cost of professional preparation ($300-$600 per year) is a tiny fraction of the potential penalty

Key Deadlines for 2026

DeadlineAction
April 15, 2026File Form 5472 + Pro Forma 1120 for tax year 2025 (or file Form 7004 for extension)
October 15, 2026Extended deadline for Form 5472 + Pro Forma 1120 (if Form 7004 was filed)
OngoingMaintain records of all reportable transactions throughout the year

How LLCPad Helps With Compliance

We built LLCPad specifically for non-resident LLC owners, and we know that compliance is one of the most stressful parts of running a US business from abroad. Here is how we help:

  • Compliance reminders: Automated alerts before every filing deadline so you never face a surprise penalty
  • EIN procurement: We handle the EIN application process so you have the foundation for all IRS filings
  • Tax professional referrals: We partner with CPAs who specialize in foreign-owned LLC tax compliance
  • Document organization: Our platform helps you keep formation documents, annual reports, and tax filings organized and accessible
  • Full formation packages: When you form your LLC through LLCPad, we make sure you understand every compliance obligation from day one — not two years later when the penalties have stacked up

Frequently Asked Questions

What if my LLC had no income and no transactions?

If your LLC truly had zero transactions — no bank deposits, no expenses paid, no capital contributions of any kind — you may not need to file. However, this situation is extremely rare. Even paying for a registered agent or LLC formation fees through the LLC creates a reportable transaction. When in doubt, file.

Do multi-member LLCs also need to file Form 5472?

Yes, if any member is a foreign person owning 25% or more. Multi-member LLCs also have additional filing requirements (Form 1065, K-1s, etc.).

What if I just close the LLC instead of filing?

Dissolving the LLC does not eliminate your filing obligation for the years it was active. You would still owe Form 5472 for every year the LLC existed and had reportable transactions.

Can the IRS actually collect penalties from foreign persons?

Yes. The IRS has multiple enforcement mechanisms, including withholding from US-source income, levying US bank accounts, and international tax treaties. Additionally, outstanding IRS penalties can affect your ability to obtain US visas and conduct business in the US.

Disclaimer: LLCPad is not a law firm, CPA firm, or tax advisory service. This guide is for informational purposes only and does not constitute tax advice. Consult a qualified tax professional for advice specific to your situation.

blogs.title: US Bank Account for Non-Residents: What Changed in 2026
blogs.excerpt: Mercury and Relay have new rules. Here's which banks still accept non-resident LLC owners and how to open an account remotely.
blogs.metaTitle: US Bank Account for Non-Residents in 2026
blogs.metaDescription: Open a US bank account as a non-resident LLC owner in 2026. Compare Mercury, Relay, and traditional banks. Updated requirements and tips.
blogs.content:
Opening a US bank account is one of the first — and most frustrating — tasks for non-resident LLC owners. You need it to accept payments, pay vendors, and establish your business as a legitimate US entity. But in 2026, the landscape has shifted considerably. Banks that were once welcoming to foreign entrepreneurs have tightened their requirements, while new options have emerged.

This guide covers your realistic options, what documents you need, and how to maximize your chances of approval — based on what is actually working right now, not outdated advice from 2023.

Why You Need a US Bank Account

Before we get into the how, let us be clear about the why. A US bank account is not optional for most foreign-owned LLCs — it is practically essential:

  • Payment processing: Stripe, PayPal, and most US payment processors require a US bank account for payouts
  • Amazon seller accounts: Amazon requires a US bank account (or a supported international bank) for disbursements
  • Business credibility: Clients and vendors take you more seriously with a US bank account on invoices
  • Tax compliance: Keeping US business income in a US account makes Form 5472 reporting much simpler. Learn about Form 5472 requirements here.
  • Clean bookkeeping: Separating business and personal finances is critical for maintaining your LLC's liability protection

The 2026 Banking Landscape: What Changed

Over the past two years, the banking environment for non-resident LLC owners has undergone some significant shifts:

Tighter KYC Requirements

Banks and fintech companies have substantially increased their Know Your Customer (KYC) and Anti-Money Laundering (AML) compliance efforts. The Corporate Transparency Act's BOI reporting requirements have made banks more cautious about onboarding foreign-owned entities. You now need more documentation, and the review process takes longer.

Mercury's Policy Changes

Mercury, once the go-to recommendation for non-resident LLC owners, has tightened its approval criteria in 2025-2026. While Mercury still accepts non-resident applicants, approval rates have dropped. Applications from certain countries face additional scrutiny, and Mercury now requires more detailed business documentation during the onboarding process. Many applicants who would have been approved in 2023 are now being rejected or asked for supplementary information.

Relay's Adjusted Stance

Relay Financial has similarly adjusted its policies. The company still serves non-residents but has introduced additional verification steps and may require a US phone number or US address verification beyond your registered agent address.

New Entrants and Alternatives

On the positive side, several newer fintech platforms have entered the market specifically targeting international entrepreneurs. The competition is creating more options, even as individual banks become pickier.

Your Options: Fintech vs Traditional Banks

Option 1: Mercury

Mercury remains one of the best options for non-resident LLC owners, despite the stricter requirements. Here is the current picture:

FeatureDetails
Accepts Non-ResidentsYes (with stricter review)
In-Person Visit RequiredNo — fully remote
Monthly FeesFree (standard account)
EIN RequiredYes
Debit CardYes (shipped internationally)
Wire TransfersDomestic and international
IntegrationStripe, QuickBooks, Xero
Approval Time1-3 weeks (up from 1-3 days)

Pros: Excellent user interface, free account, good integrations, international wire support, physical debit card shipped worldwide.

Cons: Approval is no longer guaranteed. Applications from certain high-risk countries may face additional review. Mercury has been known to close accounts with little warning if compliance concerns arise.

Option 2: Relay Financial

Relay is a strong alternative that many non-resident founders overlook:

FeatureDetails
Accepts Non-ResidentsYes (case by case)
In-Person Visit RequiredNo — fully remote
Monthly FeesFree (standard), $39/mo (Pro)
EIN RequiredYes
Multiple AccountsUp to 20 sub-accounts (free)
Debit CardYes
Wire TransfersDomestic (international via Wise integration)
Approval Time1-2 weeks

Pros: Multiple sub-accounts for organizing finances (great for separating revenue streams), profit-first budgeting tools, clean interface, good customer support.

Cons: International wire transfers require workarounds (Wise integration), non-resident approval is not automatic, limited international shipping for debit cards in some regions.

Option 3: Wise Business (Formerly TransferWise)

Wise is not a bank in the traditional sense, but it offers something valuable: US account details (routing and account numbers) that work for receiving payments.

FeatureDetails
Accepts Non-ResidentsYes (widely available)
EIN RequiredYes (for business account)
Monthly FeesFree (pay per transaction)
Multi-Currency50+ currencies
US Banking DetailsYes (ACH routing number + account number)
Debit CardYes (international shipping)
Approval Time3-7 days

Pros: Fastest approval for non-residents, multi-currency support is unmatched, excellent exchange rates, debit card available worldwide, broadly accepted by payment processors.

Cons: Not a full bank account (no checks, limited lending), some payment processors may not accept Wise account details, transaction fees on currency conversion.

Option 4: Traditional US Banks

Traditional banks like Chase, Bank of America, and Wells Fargo generally require an in-person visit to a US branch for non-resident account opening. Some community banks and credit unions are exceptions, but they are increasingly rare.

If you plan to visit the US, opening a traditional bank account in person is worth considering for the stability and full-feature banking. Bring your passport, LLC formation documents, EIN letter, and operating agreement. Call ahead to confirm the specific branch handles business accounts for non-residents.

Documents You Need (For Any Bank)

Regardless of which bank or fintech you choose, have these documents ready:

  1. Articles of Organization: The state-filed formation document for your LLC. You receive this after forming your LLC.
  2. EIN Confirmation Letter (CP 575): The official letter from the IRS confirming your Employer Identification Number. Apply for your EIN through LLCPad if you have not already.
  3. Operating Agreement: The internal governance document for your LLC. Even single-member LLCs should have one — banks want to see it.
  4. Passport: A clear color copy or scan of your passport's photo page.
  5. Proof of Address: A utility bill, bank statement, or government document showing your home address in your country of residence.
  6. Business Description: A clear explanation of what your LLC does, who your customers are, and how you generate revenue.
  7. Website or Online Presence: Banks want to verify your business is real. A website, active social media, or marketplace profile (Amazon, Shopify) strengthens your application.

The EIN Requirement: Do Not Skip This

Every bank or fintech that offers business accounts requires an EIN (Employer Identification Number). This is your LLC's tax ID number, issued by the IRS.

For non-residents without a Social Security Number, the EIN application process takes 4-6 weeks when done by mail or fax (the online application requires an SSN or ITIN). LLCPad handles this process for you as part of our LLC formation packages.

Do not attempt to open a bank account before receiving your EIN — your application will be immediately rejected.

Step-by-Step: Opening Your Account

Here is the process we recommend for the highest chance of success:

Step 1: Get Your LLC and EIN First

Form your LLC, receive your formation documents from the state, and obtain your EIN from the IRS. Have your Operating Agreement prepared and signed. This entire process typically takes 4-8 weeks.

Step 2: Set Up a Business Website

Even a simple one-page website at your business domain significantly improves your approval odds. Banks check your online presence during the review process. Make sure the website clearly describes your business and matches the information on your application.

Step 3: Apply to Multiple Banks Simultaneously

Do not put all your eggs in one basket. Apply to Mercury, Relay, and Wise Business at the same time. If one rejects you, you still have options. There is no penalty for multiple applications.

Step 4: Write a Clear Business Description

When filling out the application, describe your business in plain, specific terms. Avoid vague descriptions like "consulting" or "online business." Instead, write something like: "We sell custom phone accessories through our Shopify store and Amazon, targeting customers in the United States and Europe."

Step 5: Respond to Verification Requests Promptly

Banks will often ask follow-up questions or request additional documents. Respond within 24-48 hours with clear, complete answers. Delays in responding are one of the top reasons applications get rejected.

Step 6: Make an Initial Deposit

Once approved, fund your account promptly. An account that sits at $0 for weeks may trigger compliance flags. Even a small deposit ($100-$500) shows the account is active and legitimate.

Common Rejection Reasons (And How to Avoid Them)

Understanding why banks reject applications helps you avoid the same mistakes:

1. Vague or Inconsistent Business Description

If your application says "marketing services" but your website says "cryptocurrency consulting," that inconsistency will flag your application. Make sure all information matches across your application, website, and LLC documents.

2. High-Risk Business Categories

Certain business types face automatic additional scrutiny or outright rejection: cryptocurrency, gambling, adult content, firearms, and certain financial services. If your business touches any of these areas, be upfront about it and emphasize your compliance measures.

3. Incomplete Documentation

Submitting blurry passport scans, unsigned operating agreements, or missing documents is an easy way to get rejected. Take the time to prepare clean, complete documentation before applying.

4. No Online Presence

A business with no website, no social media, and no verifiable online presence looks suspicious to compliance teams. Even a basic LinkedIn company page or a simple website helps.

5. Country of Residence

Banks assess risk partly based on your country of residence. Applicants from countries on OFAC sanctions lists will be automatically rejected. Applicants from countries with higher perceived financial crime risk may face additional scrutiny — this is not something you can change, but you can compensate by providing thorough documentation and a clear business case.

Our Recommended Strategy for 2026

Based on current approval rates and features, here is what we recommend:

  1. Primary account: Mercury — Best all-around option for non-resident LLC owners. Full banking features, no monthly fees, good integrations.
  2. Backup account: Wise Business — Highest approval rate, excellent for multi-currency needs and international transfers. Open this alongside Mercury as a backup and for currency conversion.
  3. If Mercury rejects you: Relay — Strong alternative with unique features like sub-accounts for budgeting.
  4. If visiting the US: Open a Chase or Bank of America account — The stability of a major bank is valuable, and in-person opening has a much higher approval rate for non-residents.

Tips to Maximize Your Chances

  • Apply early in the week (Monday-Wednesday). Support teams are more responsive if issues come up.
  • Have a US phone number if possible. Google Voice works for some banks. Others may require a physical SIM or VoIP service.
  • Use a professional email address matching your business domain (e.g., yourname@yourbusiness.com), not a Gmail or Yahoo address.
  • Keep your initial application simple. If you have multiple business activities, lead with the most straightforward one.
  • Do not mention personal use. These are business accounts — frame everything in terms of your LLC's operations.
  • Be patient. Approval times have increased across the board. If your application is "under review" for two weeks, that is normal — do not open a new application or contact support repeatedly.

How LLCPad Helps

Banking is one of the most common pain points our clients face. Here is how we smooth the process:

  • Complete formation documents: When you form your LLC through LLCPad, you receive all the documents banks require — Articles of Organization, Operating Agreement, and EIN — formatted and organized for bank applications.
  • EIN procurement: Our EIN application service ensures you have the tax ID number every bank requires, without navigating the IRS process yourself.
  • Banking guidance: We provide updated recommendations on which banks are currently accepting non-resident applications and any special requirements.
  • Virtual address: If you need a US address beyond your registered agent, our virtual address service provides a physical US mailing address for your LLC.

Ready to set the foundation? Start your LLC formation today, and we will make sure you have everything you need to open a US bank account with confidence.
