blogs.title: The 2026 Remittance Tax: What Every Foreign LLC Owner Needs to Know
blogs.excerpt: The new 1% excise tax on outbound remittances could impact how you move money from your US LLC. Here's what changed and how to plan.
blogs.metaTitle: 2026 Remittance Tax: Impact on Foreign LLC Owners
blogs.metaDescription: Understand the 2026 remittance excise tax and how it affects foreign-owned US LLCs. Exemptions, planning strategies, and compliance tips.
blogs.content:
A New Tax on Moving Money Out of the United States

If you own a US LLC from abroad, you have probably heard rumblings about a new tax on money leaving the country. Starting January 1, 2026, the United States began enforcing a 1% federal excise tax on certain outbound remittance transfers under Section 4475 of the Internal Revenue Code. This was enacted as part of the One, Big, Beautiful Bill Act (Public Law 119-21), signed into law on July 4, 2025.

For foreign entrepreneurs who formed a US LLC to run an Amazon store, freelance business, or e-commerce operation, this law raises real questions. Will it cost you more to send your profits home? Are there exemptions? And what can you do to minimize the impact?

This guide breaks down the remittance tax 2026 in plain English, explains exactly who is affected, and walks you through practical strategies to keep more of your hard-earned money.

What Exactly Is the Remittance Excise Tax?

The remittance excise tax is a 1% fee levied on certain cross-border money transfers sent from the United States to recipients in foreign countries. The tax is collected by remittance transfer providers (companies like Western Union, MoneyGram, and similar services) at the point of the transaction and then deposited with the IRS on a semimonthly basis.

The key word here is "certain." Not every international transfer triggers this tax. The law specifically targets transfers funded by:

  • Cash handed to a remittance provider
  • Money orders
  • Cashier's checks
  • Other similar physical instruments

This is a critical distinction. The tax was designed primarily to capture informal, cash-based remittances rather than standard electronic banking transactions. We will get into the exemptions shortly, because they matter enormously for LLC owners.

Legislative Background: How We Got Here

The remittance tax began as a proposal within broader budget reconciliation legislation in early 2025. The original drafts floated a rate as high as 5%, which caused significant alarm among immigrant communities, expat organizations, and foreign business owners alike.

Through the legislative process, the rate was reduced from 5% to 3.5%, and then further lowered to the final 1% in the Senate version. The One, Big, Beautiful Bill Act was signed by the President on July 4, 2025, with the remittance tax provision taking effect on January 1, 2026.

The stated purpose of the tax is to generate federal revenue from outbound capital flows. Supporters argue it helps offset other tax cuts in the legislation. Critics, including the American Citizens Abroad organization, have raised concerns about its impact on lower-income remittance senders and the compliance burden on transfer providers.

Key Timeline

Date Event
Early 2025 Initial proposal at 5% rate
Mid 2025 Rate reduced to 3.5%, then 1%
July 4, 2025 One, Big, Beautiful Bill Act signed into law
January 1, 2026 Excise tax takes effect
January 29, 2026 First semimonthly deposit due from providers
Q1-Q3 2026 IRS penalty relief period for providers (Notice 2025-55)

Who Is Affected by the Remittance Tax?

Here is where things get nuanced. The foreign LLC remittance excise tax applies to any sender in the United States making a covered remittance transfer to a foreign country. This includes:

  • US citizens sending money to family abroad
  • Green card holders remitting funds overseas
  • Non-resident visa holders working in the US
  • Foreign-owned business entities transferring profits abroad

Notably, the tax does not discriminate based on citizenship or immigration status. If you are sending money from US soil through a covered method, the tax can apply. The legislation also includes anti-conduit provisions under Section 7701(l) to prevent people from routing transfers through intermediaries to avoid the tax.

Are LLC Profit Distributions Affected?

This is the question most foreign LLC owners are asking. The answer depends on how you move your profits, not whether you are moving them. If you withdraw cash from your LLC's earnings, walk into a Western Union office, and send it abroad, you would owe the 1% tax. But if you transfer those same profits electronically from your US business bank account, you are most likely exempt. More on that below.

The Exemptions That Matter Most

The good news for most foreign LLC owners is that the law includes significant exemptions that effectively remove the tax burden from standard business banking operations. The 1% excise tax does not apply when the transfer is funded by:

  • A withdrawal from a US bank or credit union account that is subject to Bank Secrecy Act (BSA) regulations
  • A US-issued debit card
  • A US-issued credit card
  • Transfers of $15 or less

In other words, if you are using your US business bank account to wire profits to your home country bank account, you are generally exempt from this tax. The exemption for BSA-regulated financial institution accounts covers the vast majority of standard banking wire transfers.

This is why having a proper US bank account for your LLC is no longer just convenient — it is a genuine financial advantage.

How the Tax Impacts Foreign LLC Profit Repatriation

Let us walk through a few common scenarios to show the practical impact:

Scenario 1: Cash-Based Transfer (Taxed)

You earn $10,000 in your LLC. You withdraw cash and send it home through a remittance service like Western Union. The provider collects a $100 excise tax (1% of $10,000) on top of their regular fees. Over a year, if you send $50,000 this way, you lose $500 to the excise tax alone.

Scenario 2: Bank Wire Transfer (Exempt)

You earn the same $10,000. You initiate an international wire transfer from your US LLC's bank account (Mercury, Relay, Chase, etc.) to your personal bank account in Bangladesh, India, or Pakistan. Because the funds are coming from a BSA-regulated bank account, the excise tax does not apply. You pay only the bank's standard wire fee, typically $15–40.

Scenario 3: Wise or Payoneer (Generally Exempt)

You use a service like Wise (formerly TransferWise) or Payoneer to move funds. If you are funding the transfer from your US bank account or using a US-issued debit card linked to your LLC, the exemption applies. The key is the funding source, not the transfer provider.

Transfer Method Subject to 1% Tax? Notes
Cash at remittance office Yes Tax collected by provider
Money order Yes Treated same as cash
Cashier's check Yes Physical instrument
Bank wire transfer No BSA-regulated account exempt
US debit card transfer No US-issued card exempt
US credit card transfer No US-issued card exempt
Wise/Payoneer from bank No Funded from BSA account

Planning Strategies for Foreign LLC Owners

Now that you understand the mechanics, here are actionable steps to ensure the remittance tax has minimal impact on your business.

1. Open a US Business Bank Account (If You Have Not Already)

This is the single most important step. A US business bank account at a BSA-regulated institution gives you an automatic exemption for all outbound transfers funded from that account. Services like Mercury, Relay, and Bluevine welcome foreign-owned LLCs. If you need help, our business banking assistance service walks you through the entire process.

2. Always Fund Transfers Electronically

Even if you use a remittance provider, make sure you are funding the transfer with a bank withdrawal or US-issued debit card rather than cash. The tax trigger is the funding method, not the service provider.

3. Consider Reinvesting in Your US Business

If you do not need profits immediately, reinvesting them into inventory, marketing, or expanding your product line keeps the money in the US and growing. This is especially relevant for Amazon sellers who can reinvest in new ASINs or PPC campaigns.

4. Consolidate Transfers

Rather than making many small transfers (which may accumulate fees even if exempt from the excise tax), consolidate into fewer, larger transfers. This reduces banking fees and simplifies your bookkeeping.

5. Keep Clean Records

Document every outbound transfer, the funding source, and the amount. If you are audited or questioned, having clear records showing your transfers were funded from a BSA-regulated account makes compliance straightforward. Our bookkeeping service can help you maintain organized financial records.

6. Stay Current on IRS Guidance

The IRS has already issued Notice 2025-55 providing penalty relief for the first three quarters of 2026. Additional guidance, clarifications, and potentially updated regulations are expected. We recommend subscribing to IRS updates or following a qualified tax professional who specializes in non-resident tax matters.

How Does This Compare to Other Countries?

The US is not the first country to tax outbound remittances, but the approach is distinctive. Here is how it compares:

Country Remittance Tax Rate Scope
United States (2026) Excise tax 1% Cash-funded remittances only
India (TCS) Tax Collected at Source 5–20% All outbound remittances over ₹7 lakh
Saudi Arabia Levy on transfers Proposed (shelved) Was planned for foreign workers
UAE None 0% No outbound remittance tax

Compared to India's Tax Collected at Source (TCS), which can reach 20% on certain foreign remittances, the US 1% excise tax is relatively modest and much narrower in scope because of the cash-only trigger. The banking exemption makes the US approach far less burdensome for established business owners who use formal financial channels.

Common Questions About the Remittance Tax

Does this tax apply to business-to-business wire transfers?

If your LLC sends a wire transfer from its US bank account to pay an overseas supplier, the transfer is funded from a BSA-regulated institution and would generally be exempt. However, the anti-conduit rules mean you cannot route personal cash-based transfers through a business entity to avoid the tax.

What if I use PayPal or Stripe to receive payments?

Receiving payments is not affected. The tax only applies to outbound transfers from the US. Your customers paying you through PayPal or Stripe is an inbound transaction and is not covered by Section 4475.

Do I need to file anything extra for this tax?

As an individual sender, no. The remittance transfer provider is responsible for collecting the tax and remitting it to the IRS. They file quarterly returns. You do not need to separately report remittance tax on your personal or business tax returns. However, you should keep records of your transfers and funding methods for your own protection.

Is there a minimum transfer amount?

Transfers of $15 or less are excluded from the tax. Beyond that threshold, the 1% applies to the full amount of any covered transfer.

How LLCPad Helps You Stay Ahead

At LLCPad, we help international entrepreneurs build their US businesses on a solid foundation. That foundation includes the banking and compliance infrastructure that makes the remittance tax largely irrelevant to your operations.

  • LLC Formation — We set up your Wyoming or New Mexico LLC with all the documentation you need to open a US bank account.
  • EIN Application — Your Employer Identification Number is required for banking and tax compliance.
  • Business Banking Assistance — We guide you through opening a US business bank account, which provides the BSA exemption from the remittance tax.
  • Bookkeeping — We keep your financial records organized so every transfer is documented and audit-ready.
  • Tax Filing — Our team ensures your LLC meets all federal and state tax obligations, including the Form 5472 required for foreign-owned single-member LLCs.

The Bottom Line

The 2026 remittance excise tax sounds alarming at first, but for foreign LLC owners who use proper banking channels, the practical impact is close to zero. The 1% tax targets cash-based, informal remittances. If you are running a legitimate US business with a US bank account, your profit transfers are exempt.

The real takeaway is this: having the right business infrastructure — an LLC, an EIN, a US bank account, and proper bookkeeping — does not just make your business look professional. It actively saves you money and keeps you compliant as laws evolve.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or tax advice. LLCPad is not a law firm or accounting firm. Consult a qualified tax professional for advice specific to your situation.

blogs.title: How to Set Up TikTok Shop in the US as a Non-Resident
blogs.excerpt: LLC, EIN, Letter of Authorization, seller verification — the complete playbook for launching your TikTok Shop from abroad in 2026.
blogs.metaTitle: TikTok Shop US Setup for Non-Residents (2026)
blogs.metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to setting up TikTok Shop in the US as a non-resident. LLC requirements, EIN, verification, and seller tips for 2026.
blogs.content:
Why TikTok Shop Is the Biggest E-Commerce Opportunity in 2026

TikTok Shop has exploded from a novelty feature into a serious sales channel. In 2025, TikTok Shop generated billions in US gross merchandise value, and 2026 is shaping up to be even bigger. The platform's algorithm-driven discovery engine means a single viral video can generate thousands of sales overnight — something that takes months of optimization on Amazon or Shopify.

For international entrepreneurs, the opportunity is massive. But there is a catch: selling on TikTok Shop in the US requires a US business entity. You cannot simply sign up with a foreign passport and start listing products. TikTok enforces strict identity verification, business documentation checks, and banking requirements.

The good news? With the right setup, non-residents can absolutely sell on TikTok Shop from abroad. This guide walks you through every step, from LLC formation to your first product listing.

The US LLC Requirement: Why You Need One

TikTok Shop's US marketplace requires sellers to register as either an individual with a US Social Security Number (SSN) or as a US-registered business entity. Since non-residents typically do not have an SSN, the business entity path is the way forward.

A US Limited Liability Company (LLC) is the preferred structure for several reasons:

  • Legal separation — Your personal assets are protected from business liabilities
  • Tax flexibility — Single-member LLCs owned by non-resident aliens can benefit from favorable tax treatment on non-US-sourced income
  • Banking access — US banks require a US business entity to open a business account
  • Platform compliance — TikTok, Amazon, Walmart, and Shopify all accept US LLCs from foreign owners

We recommend forming your LLC in Wyoming or New Mexico, as both states are friendly to non-resident owners, have no state income tax on out-of-state income, and offer strong privacy protections. Our LLC formation service handles the entire process for you.

Step-by-Step: From Zero to TikTok Shop Seller

Here is the complete roadmap. Each step builds on the previous one, so follow them in order.

Step 1: Form Your US LLC

This is the foundation. You need Articles of Organization filed with a US state, a registered agent in that state, and an Operating Agreement. The process typically takes 3–7 business days depending on the state.

Critical detail: the legal name on your Articles of Organization must exactly match what you later enter in TikTok's Seller Center, on your EIN documentation, and on your bank account. Even a small discrepancy (like "LLC" vs. "L.L.C.") can trigger a manual review that delays your application by weeks.

Start your LLC formation here — we ensure all your documents are consistent and verification-ready.

Step 2: Get Your EIN (Employer Identification Number)

Your EIN is essentially your business's tax ID number, issued by the IRS. TikTok Shop requires it during seller verification. You will receive an IRS Notice CP 575 confirming your EIN, and this is one of the documents TikTok accepts as proof of your business identity.

For non-residents without an SSN or ITIN, the EIN application is done by fax or mail (Form SS-4), which typically takes 4–6 weeks through the IRS. Our EIN application service handles the paperwork and follow-up so you do not have to navigate IRS phone lines.

Step 3: Open a US Business Bank Account

TikTok Shop deposits your earnings into a US bank account. You need one. TikTok also cross-references your banking details against your LLC name and EIN, so everything must match.

Popular options for non-resident LLC owners include:

  • Mercury — Popular with startups and e-commerce sellers; remote-friendly application
  • Relay — Free business banking with no minimum balance
  • Bluevine — Offers checking with interest

Our business banking assistance guides you through the application and ensures your documents are in order before you apply.

Step 4: Apply for an ITIN (If Needed)

While an EIN is for your business, TikTok may also require a personal tax identification number for the Primary Business Representative. If you do not have an SSN, you will need an ITIN (Individual Taxpayer Identification Number). This is used for the W-9 form that TikTok requires for tax reporting.

Step 5: Register on TikTok Seller Center

Now for the actual TikTok Shop registration. Go to seller-us.tiktok.com and select "Sign Up" as a business seller. You will need:

  • Your LLC's legal name (exactly as on your Articles of Organization)
  • Your EIN and IRS CP 575 notice
  • A US business address (your registered agent address or virtual address works)
  • US bank account details
  • A W-9 form

TikTok cross-checks three independent data points: your entity name on the Articles of Organization, the EIN on your CP 575, and the legal name on your bank account. All three must match exactly.

Step 6: Appoint a Primary Business Representative

This is the step that trips up most non-resident sellers. Every TikTok Shop needs a US-based Primary Business Representative (PBR) — a person authorized to act on behalf of the company. The PBR must provide:

  • A US government-issued photo ID (driver's license or state ID)
  • A Social Security Number
  • A live selfie video for identity verification
  • A US residential address (not a PO Box)

If you are the LLC owner but live abroad, you will need to appoint someone in the US as your PBR. This is where the Letter of Authorization (LOA) comes in.

Step 7: Prepare Your Letter of Authorization

A Letter of Authorization is a written document confirming that a person of authority (you, the LLC owner) has granted permission for another person (the PBR) to act on behalf of your business on TikTok Shop. TikTok may request this document during verification.

Your LOA should include:

  • Your LLC's full legal name and EIN
  • The name and role of the person being authorized
  • A clear statement of what they are authorized to do
  • The LLC owner's or manager's signature
  • Date of authorization

TikTok sometimes provides a template for this. If they do not, a professionally formatted letter on your company letterhead works.

Identity Verification: What to Expect

TikTok's verification process has become stricter in 2026. The platform verifies your business at multiple levels:

  1. Business Entity Verification — Confirming your LLC is real and active with your state
  2. Tax Identity Verification — Matching your EIN and W-9 information
  3. Primary Business Representative Verification — ID check, SSN verification, and live selfie
  4. Beneficial Ownership Disclosure — Any owner with 25% or more ownership must be disclosed
  5. Bank Account Verification — Confirming the account matches your business name

Typical approval time is 24–48 hours if all documents are clean and consistent. Some sellers report approval in as little as 19 hours.

Common Rejection Reasons (and How to Avoid Them)

Understanding why applications get rejected will save you weeks of frustration. Here are the most common issues:

Rejection Reason What It Means How to Fix It
Business information inconsistent Name or address mismatch between documents Ensure LLC name is identical on all documents
Uploaded documents unclear Scans were blurry, cropped, or low resolution Upload high-resolution, full-page scans
ID information does not match PBR's name or date of birth entered incorrectly Double-check all personal details against the ID
PBR address mismatch PBR's ID address does not match portal entry Use the exact address shown on the PBR's ID
Bank account name mismatch Bank account name differs from LLC legal name Open bank account using exact LLC legal name

The golden rule: consistency is everything. Before submitting your application, lay out your Articles of Organization, CP 575 letter, bank statement, and W-9 side by side. The business name, address, and EIN should be identical across all of them.

Fulfillment Options: FBT vs. Third-Party Logistics

Once approved, you need to figure out how to actually get products to customers. As a non-resident seller, you have two main options.

Fulfilled by TikTok (FBT)

FBT is TikTok's in-house fulfillment service, similar to Amazon's FBA. You ship inventory to TikTok's fulfillment centers, and they handle storage, picking, packing, and shipping.

  • Pros: Faster delivery (3-day delivery badge boosts visibility), 20–35% shipping savings, 60 days free storage, reduced operational burden
  • Cons: Less control over packaging and branding, storage fees increase after 60 days, limited value-added services
  • Costs: Starting at $3.58 per single-unit order and $2.86 per item for multi-unit orders (as of January 2026)

Third-Party Logistics (3PL)

A 3PL warehouse receives, stores, and ships your products on your behalf. Popular 3PLs for TikTok Shop sellers include ShipBob, ShipHero, and Deliverr.

  • Pros: Custom packaging and inserts, multi-channel fulfillment (sell on TikTok, Amazon, and Shopify from one warehouse), more flexible storage terms, often lower long-term storage costs
  • Cons: No "Free 3-Day Delivery" badge, may have higher per-order costs for small volumes, requires more setup

For sellers just starting out, FBT is the easier choice. The free 3-day delivery badge significantly improves your product's visibility in TikTok's algorithm. As your business grows and you want more control over the customer experience, transitioning to a 3PL gives you more flexibility.

Product Listing Tips for TikTok Shop Success

Getting approved is half the battle. Here are practical tips to make your products actually sell.

Optimize for Discovery

  • Use keyword-rich product titles that match what shoppers search for on TikTok
  • Write detailed product descriptions with benefits, not just features
  • Upload at least 5 high-quality product images with white backgrounds and lifestyle shots
  • Add a product video (even a simple 15-second demo) — listings with videos get significantly more engagement

Price Competitively

TikTok shoppers expect deals. Factor in TikTok's 6% referral fee (3% for your first 30 days), fulfillment costs, and your product cost to set a price that is competitive while still profitable. The sweet spot for TikTok Shop products tends to be $15–$50.

Leverage Creator Partnerships

TikTok Shop's affiliate program lets creators promote your products in their videos. You set a commission rate, and creators who drive sales earn that commission. This is one of the most powerful features of TikTok Shop and costs you nothing unless a sale is made.

TikTok Shop Seller Fees at a Glance

Fee Type Rate Notes
Referral fee (most categories) 6% 3% promotional rate for first 30 days
Referral fee (jewelry/collectibles) 5% Category-specific rate
FBT fulfillment (single unit) From $3.58 Varies by weight tier
FBT fulfillment (multi-unit) From $2.86/item Up to 24% discount on multi-unit orders
FBT storage (first 60 days) Free Fees apply after 60 days
Affiliate commission You set the rate Typically 10–20% for creator partnerships

The Numbers That Should Excite You

TikTok Shop is still in its growth phase in the US, which means less competition and more algorithmic support for new sellers compared to saturated marketplaces like Amazon. Consider these facts:

  • TikTok has over 170 million US users, and shopping features are integrated directly into the For You feed
  • The average TikTok user spends 95 minutes per day on the app — that is 95 minutes of potential product discovery
  • New sellers get a promotional 3% referral rate for their first 30 days, saving you half the standard fee
  • Products with the FBT 3-day delivery badge see measurably higher conversion rates

How LLCPad Makes This Entire Process Simple

Setting up a TikTok Shop from abroad involves multiple moving pieces: LLC formation, EIN, banking, ITIN, and then the TikTok application itself. At LLCPad, we have helped hundreds of international entrepreneurs launch on US e-commerce platforms, and we offer a streamlined path:

  • LLC Formation — We file your LLC and ensure all documents are consistent for platform verification
  • EIN Application — We handle the IRS paperwork and get your CP 575 notice
  • Registered Agent — A US address for your LLC's legal correspondence
  • Virtual Address — A US business address for your TikTok Shop profile
  • Business Banking — We guide you through opening a US bank account remotely
  • ITIN Application — For personal tax ID when you do not have an SSN
  • TikTok Shop Setup — We walk you through the entire seller registration and verification process

Ready to Start Selling?

TikTok Shop represents a rare window of opportunity for international sellers willing to do the setup work. The platform is actively recruiting sellers, offering promotional fee rates, and investing heavily in its fulfillment infrastructure. The sellers who get in now, while the marketplace is still growing, will have a significant advantage over those who wait.

The setup process is not trivial — it requires a real US business entity, proper documentation, and attention to detail during verification. But with the right guidance, it is absolutely achievable from anywhere in the world.

Start your LLC formation today and take the first step toward building your TikTok Shop business.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or tax advice. LLCPad is not a law firm. Platform requirements and fees may change. Always verify current requirements directly with TikTok Shop's Seller Center.

legalPages.title: Privacy Policy
legalPages.content:
1. Information We Collect

We collect information that you provide directly to us, including:

  • Personal Information: Name, email address, phone number, mailing address
  • Business Information: LLC name, business address, EIN, member information
  • Payment Information: Credit card details, billing address
  • Identity Documents: Passport or ID copies (for compliance purposes)

2. How We Use Your Information

We use the information we collect to:

  • Process your orders and form your business entities
  • Communicate with you about your orders
  • Send important updates and compliance reminders
  • Improve our services and customer experience
  • Comply with legal obligations

3. Information Sharing

We may share your information with:

  • State Agencies: Required for filing your business documents
  • IRS: For EIN applications
  • Service Providers: Who assist in providing our services
  • Legal Requirements: When required by law

We do NOT sell your personal information to third parties.

4. Data Security

We implement appropriate security measures to protect your personal information, including:

  • SSL encryption for all data transmission
  • Secure data storage with access controls
  • Regular security audits and updates

5. Your Rights

You have the right to:

  • Access your personal information
  • Request correction of inaccurate data
  • Request deletion of your data (subject to legal requirements)
  • Opt-out of marketing communications

6. Contact Us

For privacy-related inquiries, please contact us at privacy@llcpad.com

legalPages.title: Disclaimer
legalPages.content:
Not a Law Firm

LLCPad is NOT a law firm and does not provide legal advice. We are a business formation document filing service. The information provided on this website and through our services is for general informational purposes only.

No Attorney-Client Relationship

Use of our services does not create an attorney-client relationship. For legal advice, please consult with a licensed attorney in your jurisdiction who can provide advice tailored to your specific situation.

Accuracy of Information

While we strive to provide accurate and up-to-date information, we make no representations or warranties about:

  • The completeness or accuracy of information on this website
  • The suitability of our services for your specific needs
  • The outcomes of any business formation or filing

Third-Party Services

We work with various government agencies and third-party service providers. We are not responsible for:

  • Delays caused by state agencies
  • Changes in state filing requirements or fees
  • Actions or inactions of third-party service providers

Business Decisions

The decision to form a business entity, the type of entity to form, and the state of formation are important decisions that should be made after careful consideration and, where appropriate, consultation with legal and tax professionals.

Tax Advice

We do not provide tax advice. Please consult with a qualified tax professional for advice on tax matters related to your business.

Limitation of Liability

To the maximum extent permitted by law, LLCPad shall not be liable for any indirect, incidental, special, consequential, or punitive damages arising from your use of our services.

Contact Us

If you have any questions about this disclaimer, please contact us at legal@llcpad.com

legalPages.title: Refund Policy
legalPages.content:
Our Commitment

At LLCPad, we strive to provide excellent service. We understand that circumstances may change, and we've created this refund policy to be fair to our customers.

Before Filing

If your documents have NOT been filed with the state:

  • Full refund of LLCPad service fees
  • Simply contact us to cancel your order
  • Refunds processed within 5-7 business days

After Filing

If your documents HAVE been filed with the state:

  • State filing fees are non-refundable (paid directly to the state)
  • LLCPad service fees may be partially refunded on a case-by-case basis
  • We cannot reverse filings made with state agencies

Non-Refundable Items

The following are generally non-refundable:

  • State filing fees (these go directly to the state)
  • Expedited processing fees once expedited service has begun
  • Registered Agent fees after service activation
  • Third-party fees (IRS, state agencies, etc.)

How to Request a Refund

  1. Contact our support team at support@llcpad.com
  2. Provide your order number
  3. Explain the reason for your refund request
  4. Our team will review and respond within 48 hours

Processing Time

Approved refunds are processed within 5-7 business days. The time for the refund to appear in your account depends on your payment method and financial institution.

Questions?

If you have any questions about our refund policy, please contact us at support@llcpad.com
